content: Log entries must include: date, engagement/SOW reference, work items completed, stakeholder interactions, deliverables produced, and repository touches. Omit fields that don't apply — don't pad with placeholder text.
Git is the source of truth for engagement data. Database and generated reports are derived views. When in conflict, the .scribe/*.json files in the repository win.

Weekly Action Report (WAR) structure: Personnel on project, Travel, Goals for the week, Accomplishments by category, Challenges with mitigations, Technical debt, Next steps. Date range must be the actual calendar week — verify with chronos if available.

Monthly summary structure: Executive Summary (3-5 sentences), Key Metrics (hours, deliverables, milestones), Deliverables Completed, Work Breakdown by Category, Challenges and Mitigations, Risk Register updates.
Report output naming convention: YYYY-MM-DD_[ReportType]_[EngagementName]. Example: 2026-05-01_WAR_Acme-Corp.md

Hard-stop gates (evaluate first, do not proceed if any fail): set-aside type conflicts with org certifications, award method incompatible with org type, contract type requires accounting systems org lacks, physical SCIF required but org has none, contract vehicle the org cannot access.

Evaluation phases in order: (0) hard stops, (0.5) amendment currency check, (1) metadata and criteria, (1.5) timeline feasibility, (2) clearance/facility, (3) past performance match, (4) capability gap analysis, (5) key personnel, (6) OCI risk, (7) incumbent intelligence, (8) weighted scoring and recommendation, (9) missed items checklist, (10) export.
Timeline flags: RFP/RFQ with fewer than 14 days to submission is a flag; fewer than 7 days is critical. Surface explicitly in assessment output.

Incumbent intelligence sources in priority order: usaspending.gov (search by agency + NAICS + keywords), then FPDS as fallback. Record incumbent name, contract number, period of performance, and estimated value.

Weighted scoring recommendation: PURSUE = strong capability match, no hard stops, manageable risk. PURSUE WITH CONDITIONS = capability gaps or risk items that can be mitigated. DO NOT PURSUE = hard stop triggered or risk/gap profile too high.

Source material hierarchy when drafting: (1) solicitation document, (2) opportunity evaluation, (3) past performance files, (4) capability statements, (5) domain knowledge. Do not use lower-priority sources to fill gaps that higher-priority sources should cover.
Produce an outline matched to the solicitation's section structure before drafting any section. Get confirmation before proceeding to full drafts. Complete sections in order — do not jump ahead.

Past performance citations must reference verifiable work. Never use a capability claim as a past performance reference. When past performance files are available, match references to them explicitly.
Tool recommendations in technical proposals: name three specific tools with concrete rationale, include at least one open-source option. Avoid generic vendor names without specifics.
Professional reports use sentence case for headings, Oxford comma, active voice. Avoid: 'leverage', 'utilize', 'synergy', 'robust', 'seamless'. Prefer: 'use', 'apply', 'integrate'.
Status indicators in reports: ✓ Complete, ⚠ At Risk, ✗ Blocked, → In Progress. Use consistently across all report types.
When Scribe MCP is available, prefer MCP tools over reading .scribe/*.json files directly. MCP tools provide validated, access-controlled views of engagement data.
Scribe MCP authenticates via GitHub token. If tools are unavailable, check SCRIBE_GITHUB_TOKEN is set and the user is a member of the org that owns the Scribe instance.
